A:
`return p;` instead of `return h;`.

At the end of the loop `h` is always `null` (that's the loop's exit condition), so the original returns nothing useful. The reversed list's new head is `p`, which trails one step behind `h` and lands on the last non-null node.

```javascript
function rev(h){let p=null;while(h){const n=h.next;h.next=p;p=h;h=n;}return p;}
```

Trace on `1‚Üí2‚Üí3`: after the loop `p` points at `3‚Üí2‚Üí1` and `h` is `null`. Returning `p` gives the fully reversed list with the old tail (`3`) as the new head ‚Äî nothing is lost; the original code just returned the wrong variable.
S1: `function rev(h){let p=null;while(h){const n=h.next;h.next=p;p=h;h=n;}return p;}` ‚Äî fixed (`return p` not `h`): input 1‚Üí2‚Üí3 gives 3‚Üí2‚Üí1 (p ends at old tail 3, h ends null); O(n) time, O(1) space.
L:
`return p;` instead of `return h;`.

The loop condition is `while(h)`, so the loop exits only when `h` is falsy ‚Äî i.e. `h` is `null` at the end every time. That's why the original `return h` always returns `null`. The new head is `p`, which trails one node behind `h` and therefore ends on the last non-null node visited.

```javascript
function rev(h){let p=null;while(h){const n=h.next;h.next=p;p=h;h=n;}return p;}
```

Shown trace on `1‚Üí2‚Üí3` (start: `p=null`, `h=1`):

| iteration | `n=h.next` | link set (`h.next=p`) | `p` after | `h` after |
|-----------|-----------|------------------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | `2` | `1.next=null` | `1` | `2` |
| 2 | `3` | `2.next=1` | `2` | `3` |
| 3 | `null` | `3.next=2` | `3` | `null` |

After iteration 3, `h` is `null`, so the loop exits. The links now form `3‚Üí2‚Üí1‚Üínull`, and `p=3`. Returning `p` yields head `3` with all three original nodes (`1`, `2`, `3`) still reachable, so the old tail is not lost ‚Äî the original code merely returned the wrong variable.
